You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for an Uber/Amazon-style senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD system prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, with all the prescribed sub-structure, emoji markers (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓), and the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — the real root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes, NO §1–§9-LLD structure. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code that ever appears: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in the API section, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers exactly — only the rounded results are surfaced in the markdown, never unrun code. REQUIRED SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, in this order — match the headings): ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) — 🎬 structure pitch opener; 3-5 functional flows "above the line" each with feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why it's hard; "Below the line (out of scope)" list; NFRs each with a talkable target (p99 ms, 9s) + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks without it; close with 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY. ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions — each: spoken question, 🗣️ why I'm asking, ↔️ design fork (If YES.../If NO...), Assumption I'll proceed with; end with 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE the arithmetic by writing a capacity.py and running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py — paste the rounded results. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold. Plain-text math only (~, x, ÷, no LaTeX). End with a short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — per entity: name + what it owns, 🗣️ simple analogy, ⚠️ interviewer probe + one-line answer; 🎙️ Script. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+ why this verb), 2-4 key request fields (+ why; call out server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example, one key design decision (idempotency key etc.); security note (caller from JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted); 🗣️ key-terms gloss block; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing line; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" mermaid flowchart TD that is the reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (Entity | key fields with PK/shard key | chosen store | partition key | consistency); PART B a storage decision card per distinct store (what it is / why this — the access pattern that forces it / considered & rejected / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table. 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating Bad/Good/Great; each tier: ↩️ what previous got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach (mechanism) / ⚠️ what breaks (with concrete numbers) / 🔁 what forces the upgrade; Good & Great get a tier-specific mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision-forcing math + ✅ failure matrix. Each ends with 🎙️ Script + 🧠 "If they ask". Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, failure behavior. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s per path + human downtime + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | what breaks | how it degrades gracefully — what USER SEES | recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define both in plain words first, then table per data class); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + why + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards "🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]": what we chose & why / what we gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ how to say it. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 each, 12-18 total; each: ❓ question verbatim, the mechanism (2-3 sentences, terms glossed), 🗣️ in plain words, 💬 one-liner to say. End with a 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right; HLD has MANY diagrams so this matters a lot): - Use ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT to "flowchart TD" for architecture/multi-path (NEVER flowchart LR for multi-path — it stretches off screen; LR only for a trivial 3-4 node linear chain). Use sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of these breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces { }. A semicolon ';' or colon ':' or parens inside a label/message BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks inside a node, never a raw newline. - Edge labels: short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| (text inside the pipes must itself avoid the breaking chars above — no ':' or ';' or parens inside it). - Same component = same node id/label across every diagram. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram. - Each §6.x slice gets its own diagram; §6 ends with one "Final (high-level)" union diagram; deep-dive Good/Great tiers get mechanism diagrams. Aim for 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . It must be LONG and comprehensive (the md must be > 20,000 chars; realistically 40-55K). Pure markdown — tables with real line breaks, fenced code only for the JSON API examples and mermaid. No LaTeX. Emoji markers as prescribed. - You MAY create a scratch workspace dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ to write capacity.py and run it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Do NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterwards. Before finishing: (1) verify capacity.py actually runs and you used its real computed numbers; (2) self-check that all 11 section headings are present and the md is > 20K chars; (3) count your ```mermaid blocks and make sure each one's labels are free of the breaking characters listed above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: e20d25e8-c990-40f3-9f7c-87018d8fc5bd TITLE: Design Instagram PROBLEM BRIEF (this is what transcript[0] will say; frame §1 around it): A photo/video sharing social network: upload media, follow users, a home feed of followees' posts, likes/comments. Focus on the feed (fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read), media storage + CDN, and the celebrity/hot-key problem. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these — this is what makes the session strong): feed fan-out (write vs read hybrid), blob storage + CDN for media, the celebrity fan-out problem (millions of followers), read-heavy ratio. Deep dives: feed generation at scale, hot-key celebrity, media upload pipeline + CDN, consistency of likes/counts. Assume an Amazon/Uber senior-SDE (SDE-3 / L5) bar: cost & operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer, decide and move. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/e20d25e8-c990-40f3-9f7c-87018d8fc5bd_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first, write+run capacity.py for the numbers, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars = actual char count, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. API / Interface 335 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 336 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 337 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 338 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 339 ``` 340 // Request 341 POST /v1/orders 342 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 343 344 // Response 200 345 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 346 ``` 347 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 348 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 349 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 350 351 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 352 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 353 ### 6.x "" 354 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 355 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 356 357 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 358 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 359 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 360 361 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 362 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 363 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 364 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 365 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 366 367 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 368 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 369 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 370 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 371 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 372 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 373 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 374 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 375 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 376 377 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 378 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 379 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 380 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 381 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 382 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 383 384 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 385 386 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 387 388 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 389 390 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 391 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 392 393 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 394 395 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 396 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 397 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 398 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 399 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 400 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 401 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 402 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 403 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 404 405 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 406 407 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 408 409 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 410 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 411 412 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 413 414 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 415 416 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 417 418 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 419 420 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 421 422 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 423 424 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 425 426 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 427 428 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 429 430 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 431 432 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 433 434 Each deep dive ends with: 435 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 436 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 437 438 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 439 440 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 441 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 442 443 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 444 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 445 446 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 447 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 448 449 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 450 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 451 452 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 453 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 454 455 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 456 457 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 458 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 459 460 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 461 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 462 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 463 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 464 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 465 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 466 467 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 468 469 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 470 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 471 472 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 473 474 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 475 476 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 477 478 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 479 480 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 481 482 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 483 484 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 485 486

content.text: I've read both files. Now I understand the structure. Let me set up the workspace and write the capacity computation script first.

input.description: Create HLD workspace dir and verify venv python

input.content:
"""Capacity math for Design Instagram (HLD). Run with the venv python.
All outputs rounded to talkable phrases in the markdown."""

SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400

def r(n):
    """round to a talkable order of magnitude string"""
    if n >= 1e12: return f"{n/1e12:.1f}T"
    if n >= 1e9:  return f"{n/1e9:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1e6:  return f"{n/1e6:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1e3:  return f"{n/1e3:.1f}K"
    return f"{n:.0f}"

print("=" * 60)
print("DESIGN INSTAGRAM — CAPACITY")
print("=" * 60)

# ---- Users ----
total_users = 2_000_000_000        # 2B registered accounts
dau = 500_000_000                  # 500M daily active
print(f"\nTotal users: {r(total_users)}  DAU: {r(dau)}")

# ---- Feed reads (the read hot path) ----
# Each DAU opens the app and scrolls; assume ~20 feed-page fetches/day
# (open + refresh + scroll-loads), each fetch returns a page of posts.
feed_fetches_per_user = 20
feed_reads_day = dau * feed_fetches_per_user
feed_qps = feed_reads_day / SEC_PER_DAY
feed_qps_peak = feed_qps * 3        # peak-to-average ~3x
print(f"\nFeed fetches/day: {r(feed_reads_day)}")
print(f"Feed read QPS avg: {r(feed_qps)}  ({feed_qps:,.0f}/s)")
print(f"Feed read QPS peak (3x): {r(feed_qps_peak)}  ({feed_qps_peak:,.0f}/s)")

# ---- Writes (posts uploaded) ----
# ~5% of DAU post something each day, ~1.2 posts each
poster_frac = 0.05
posts_per_poster = 1.2
posts_day = dau * poster_frac * posts_per_poster
post_qps = posts_day / SEC_PER_DAY
post_qps_peak = post_qps * 3
print(f"\nPosts/day: {r(posts_day)}")
print(f"Post (write) QPS avg: {r(post_qps)}  ({post_qps:,.0f}/s)")
print(f"Post (write) QPS peak (3x): {r(post_qps_peak)}  ({post_qps_peak:,.0f}/s)")

# ---- Read:write ratio (THE decision-forcing ratio) ----
ratio = feed_reads_day / posts_day
print(f"\nREAD:WRITE ratio = {ratio:,.0f} : 1  -> ~{round(ratio,-2):,.0f}:1")

# ---- Likes & comments (interaction writes) ----
likes_per_dau = 10
likes_day = dau * likes_per_dau
likes_qps = likes_day / SEC_PER_DAY
likes_qps_peak = likes_qps * 3
print(f"\nLikes/day: {r(likes_day)}  Like QPS avg: {r(likes_qps)}  peak: {r(likes_qps_peak)} ({likes_qps_peak:,.0f}/s)")

comments_per_dau = 1
comments_day = dau * comments_per_dau
comments_qps = comments_day / SEC_PER_DAY
print(f"Comments/day: {r(comments_day)}  Comment QPS avg: {r(comments_qps)}")

# ---- Fan-out write amplification ----
# Average followers (median is tiny, mean ~200 because of celebs)
avg_followers = 200
fanout_writes_day = posts_day * avg_followers   # naive fan-out-on-write
fanout_qps = fanout_writes_day / SEC_PER_DAY
fanout_qps_peak = fanout_qps * 3
print(f"\n-- FAN-OUT ON WRITE (naive) --")
print(f"Avg followers: {avg_followers}")
print(f"Fan-out feed inserts/day: {r(fanout_writes_day)}")
print(f"Fan-out insert QPS avg: {r(fanout_qps)}  ({fanout_qps:,.0f}/s)")
print(f"Fan-out insert QPS peak: {r(fanout_qps_peak)}  ({fanout_qps_peak:,.0f}/s)")

# ---- Celebrity worst case (the hot-key problem) ----
celeb_followers = 400_000_000   # top accounts ~ hundreds of millions
# one celeb post under naive fan-out:
print(f"\n-- CELEBRITY FAN-OUT (hot key) --")
print(f"Top-account followers: {r(celeb_followers)}")
print(f"ONE celeb post -> {r(celeb_followers)} feed inserts in one fan-out job")
# if we wanted it delivered in 60s:
inserts_per_sec_60s = celeb_followers / 60
print(f"To deliver in 60s: {r(inserts_per_sec_60s)} inserts/s ({inserts_per_sec_60s:,.0f}/s) from ONE post")

# ---- Media storage (blob) ----
# avg media size after server-side transcode (a post may have several renditions)
photos_frac = 0.8
video_frac = 0.2
photo_size_mb = 1.5   # original + thumbnails averaged per photo post
video_size_mb = 15.0  # several renditions per short video averaged
avg_post_media_mb = photos_frac * photo_size_mb + video_frac * video_size_mb
media_per_day_tb = posts_day * avg_post_media_mb / 1_000_000  # MB -> TB
media_per_year_pb = media_per_day_tb * 365 / 1000
# with replication factor 3 (object store erasure ~1.4x effective, but model RF for talkability)
replication = 1.4
media_year_pb_eff = media_per_year_pb * replication
print(f"\n-- MEDIA STORAGE --")
print(f"Avg media bytes/post (blended): {avg_post_media_mb:.1f} MB")
print(f"Media ingested/day: {media_per_day_tb:,.0f} TB ~ {media_per_day_tb/1000:.1f} PB")
print(f"Media/year (raw): {media_per_year_pb:.1f} PB")
print(f"Media/year (x{replication} durability): {media_year_pb_eff:.1f} PB")

# ---- Metadata storage (posts table) ----
post_row_bytes = 1_000      # id, author, caption, media refs, ts, counters
posts_total_year = posts_day * 365
meta_year_tb = posts_total_year * post_row_bytes / 1e12
meta_year_tb_rf3 = meta_year_tb * 3   # RF3
print(f"\n-- METADATA (posts) --")
print(f"Posts/year: {r(posts_total_year)}  row ~{post_row_bytes}B")
print(f"Posts metadata/year (RF3): {meta_year_tb_rf3:,.0f} TB")

# ---- CDN egress (the real money) ----
# read media views/day ~ feed reads * posts-per-page * media-per-post (mostly served by CDN)
posts_per_page = 10
media_views_day = feed_reads_day * posts_per_page
# bytes per media view at display size (thumbnail/feed rendition, not original)
display_size_mb = 0.3
egress_per_day_pb = media_views_day * display_size_mb / 1_000_000_000  # MB -> PB
egress_per_month_pb = egress_per_day_pb * 30
cdn_offload = 0.95   # 95% served from edge
# cost: CDN egress ~ $0.02/GB blended at scale (committed)
cdn_cost_per_gb = 0.02
egress_gb_month = egress_per_month_pb * 1e6  # PB -> GB (1 PB = 1e6 GB)
cdn_cost_month = egress_gb_month * cdn_cost_per_gb
print(f"\n-- CDN EGRESS (cost driver) --")
print(f"Media views/day: {r(media_views_day)}")

input.content:
# Design Instagram — High-Level Design

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the feed fan-out, the celebrity hot-key, and the media pipeline — checking in with you as I go. I'll target an Amazon-style bar, so I'll keep cost and operations first-class and work backwards from what the user actually feels. Sound good?"

The core tension of Instagram is this: it is one of the most **read-heavy** systems ever built — people scroll far more than they post — and most of every byte served is **media** (photos and short videos), not text. So the design lives or dies on two things: how cheaply and fast we can assemble a personalized **home feed**, and how cheaply we can store and serve **blobs** through a CDN. The villain of the whole story is the **celebrity** — one post that has to reach hundreds of millions of feeds.

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 💬 "Let me frame the core tension first: this is a read-heavy, media-heavy social graph. The interesting problems aren't the upload itself — they're assembling a personalized feed at hundreds of thousands of reads a second, and serving petabytes of media without going bankrupt on bandwidth."

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**1. Upload media (create a post).** A user uploads one or more photos/short videos with a caption.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Someone picks a photo from their phone, adds a caption, taps share, and within a second or two it shows up on their profile and starts heading out to their followers."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the file is large and goes to blob storage, but it also needs server-side transcoding into multiple sizes, and the moment it's saved we have to decide who gets it in their feed — that fan-out is the whole game.

**2. Follow / unfollow users.** A user follows others to build their feed graph.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You tap follow on someone and from then on their posts can appear in your home feed."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the follow graph is enormously skewed — most people follow a few hundred accounts, but a few accounts are followed by hundreds of millions. That skew is what breaks naive designs.

**3. Home feed (timeline of followees' posts).** A user opens the app and sees a reverse-chronological-ish, ranked feed of recent posts from people they follow.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You open the app and instantly see the latest posts from everyone you follow, freshest and most relevant near the top."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the read hot path at ~350K requests/s peak. We can't query every followee's posts on every open at that scale, so we pre-compute — but pre-computing for a celebrity's 400M followers is a write bomb.

**4. Like and comment on posts (with counts).** A user likes/comments; everyone sees the counts.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You double-tap to like, the heart count ticks up, and others see roughly how many people liked it."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** a viral post can take hundreds of thousands of likes per second — you cannot do `UPDATE posts SET likes = likes + 1` on a single row at that rate; that single row is a hot key.

### Below the line (out of scope)

- Stories / Reels ranking ML, Explore/Discovery feed, DMs, ads & monetization, live video, search, notifications fan-out (mention only), content moderation ML pipeline (we'll assume an async scanning hook), and the full ranking model (we'll treat ranking as a pluggable scorer).

### Non-Functional

**A. Feed read latency — p99 < 200 ms.** Home feed open must feel instant.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When you open the app, 99 out of 100 times the feed is on screen in under a fifth of a second."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the app feels laggy, engagement drops, and a slow feed is the single most-felt failure in the whole product.

**B. Availability — feed/read path 99.99%; write/upload path 99.9%.** Reads must almost never fail.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Four nines means the read path is down less than about 52 minutes a year total; three nines on uploads means under ~8.7 hours a year — uploads can retry, reads can't wait."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if the feed errors out, hundreds of millions of sessions hit a blank screen — that's the headline incident.

**C. Durability of media & posts — 11 nines (no lost photos).** A user's photos must never disappear.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once you see 'posted,' that photo is effectively never lost — the chance of losing it is astronomically small."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** losing user memories is unforgivable and the top trust-killer for a photo product.

**D. Feed freshness — new posts visible within seconds to minutes.** Eventual consistency is fine for the feed.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you post now, your followers see it within a few seconds normally, and within a minute or two even for a celebrity — it doesn't have to be instant for everyone."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if we demanded instant consistency for every follower we'd be forced into synchronous fan-out and the write path would melt.

**E. Cost efficiency — CDN egress is the budget.** The design must be operable at a sane $/month.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Serving the images is the biggest bill by far — bigger than all the databases combined — so every design choice that reduces bytes-over-the-wire is worth real money."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** at ~900 PB/month of egress, a careless rendition or cache strategy turns an $18M/mo bill into a $50M/mo bill.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** ranking quality (the ML model) and abuse/moderation are real, hard, and I'm treating them as pluggable hooks here, not solving them — I'll call that out in the ledger.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at the core, users upload media, follow people, read a home feed, and like/comment. The system is overwhelmingly read-heavy and media-heavy. My non-functionals are: feed reads under 200 ms at p99, four-nines on the read path, eleven-nines durability so we never lose a photo, feed freshness measured in seconds-to-minutes — eventual consistency is fine here — and cost, because CDN egress dwarfs everything else. I'm scoping out stories, DMs, ads, and the ranking model itself, treating ranking as a pluggable scorer."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "What's the scale — how many daily active users, and what's the read-to-write ratio you're expecting?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Because if it's a million users I build a monolith and go home; if it's hundreds of millions and 99% of traffic is reads, the entire design bends toward pre-computing feeds and caching media."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If LARGE (100s of millions DAU) → fan-out + heavy caching + CDN, sharding everywhere. If SMALL → single Postgres, compute feed on read, skip the fan-out machinery entirely.
- **Assumption:** Instagram-scale — ~500M DAU, ~2B accounts, read:write around 300:1.

**Q2 — "How fresh does the feed need to be? Is reverse-chronological enough, or do we need ML ranking?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Freshness decides whether I can fan-out on write — pre-build feeds — or whether ranking forces me to assemble fresh at read time."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If seconds-to-minutes freshness + simple ranking is OK → fan-out-on-write works. If we need real-time, per-request heavy ML ranking → I lean toward read-time assembly with a candidate-generation + ranking layer.
- **Assumption:** Freshness of seconds-to-minutes is fine; ranking is a re-ordering step on top of a pre-assembled candidate set.

**Q3 — "How extreme is the follower skew — are there accounts with hundreds of millions of followers?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "This is the make-or-break question. If everyone has a few hundred followers, naive fan-out works. If a few accounts have 400 million followers, fan-out-on-write for them is a write bomb and I need a hybrid."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If skew is extreme (yes) → hybrid: fan-out-on-write for normal users, fan-out-on-read (pull) for celebrities. If skew is flat → pure fan-out-on-write.
- **Assumption:** Extreme skew — top accounts have hundreds of millions of followers. Hybrid is mandatory.

**Q4 — "Photos only, or short videos too? And do we do server-side transcoding?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Video changes storage and bandwidth by an order of magnitude and forces an async transcoding pipeline; it dominates the cost conversation."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If video → async transcode pipeline + adaptive bitrate renditions + bigger CDN bill. If photo-only → simpler, just generate thumbnails.
- **Assumption:** Mostly photos (~80%) plus short videos (~20%); server-side transcoding into multiple renditions.

**Q5 — "Do like/comment counts need to be exact, or is 'approximately 1.2M likes' acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Exact counts on a viral post mean a single hot row taking 100K+ writes/s — impossible on one row. Approximate counts let me shard the counter."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If approximate is OK → sharded/probabilistic counters, eventual. If exact required → much harder, materialized aggregation with reconciliation.
- **Assumption:** Approximate, eventually-consistent counts are fine for display; the user's own like state must be read-your-own-writes correct.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — Instagram-scale, extreme follower skew, video included, eventual feed and counts. Are you good with me proceeding on those, or do you want to pin any of them differently before I size it?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

> First, glosses: **DAU** — daily active users, people who open the app on a given day. **QPS** — queries per second, how many requests hit the backend each second. **Fan-out** — copying one post into many followers' feeds. **Egress** — bytes leaving our network to users' devices (what the CDN bills us for). I computed these with a `capacity.py` I ran; below are the rounded results and the math that produced them.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed read QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 20 feed fetches/day = 10B reads/day; ÷ 86,400 s ≈ 116K/s avg; x3 peak ≈ 347K/s | ~350K/s | "about 350 thousand feed reads a second" | This is THE hot path — pre-compute + cache, don't query live |
| Post write QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 5% post x 1.2 posts = 30M/day; ÷ 86,400 ≈ 347/s avg; x3 ≈ 1K/s | ~1K/s | "about a thousand posts a second" | Writes are tiny — we can afford expensive work per write |
| Read:write ratio | 10B reads ÷ 30M writes | ~300:1 | "roughly 300 reads for every write" | Spend the budget on reads; do work on the write side to make reads cheap |
| Naive fan-out inserts (peak) | 30M posts/day x 200 avg followers = 6B inserts/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 69K/s avg; x3 ≈ 208K/s | ~210K/s | "about 200 thousand feed inserts a second" | Fan-out-on-write is feasible for normal users |
| Celebrity single-post fan-out | one post x 400M followers | ~400M inserts | "one celeb post is 400 million feed writes" | Forces the hybrid — NEVER fan-out a celebrity on write |
| Like QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 10 likes/day = 5B/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 58K/s; x3 ≈ 174K/s | ~175K/s | "about 175 thousand likes a second" | Counters must be sharded, never a single hot row |
| Media ingested/year | 30M posts/day x ~4.2 MB blended x 365, x1.4 durability | ~64 PB/yr | "tens of petabytes of new media a year" | Object store (S3-class) + lifecycle tiering, not a DB |
| CDN egress/month | 10B reads x 10 posts/page x 0.3 MB display = 30 PB/day; x30 ≈ 900 PB/mo | ~900 PB/mo | "roughly 900 petabytes out the door a month" | The dominant cost line — ~$18M/mo; optimize bytes-on-wire |
| Feed-store miss QPS (peak) | 347K/s peak x (1 − 0.95 hit) ≈ 17K/s residual to the store | ~17K/s | "about 17 thousand reads a second hit the store" | A handful of cache shards absorb the read tide |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **celebrity single-post fan-out of ~400M writes**. Its flip threshold: if no account exceeded roughly **the point where fan-out latency blows past our freshness SLO** — practically, more than a few hundred thousand to a million followers — we'd push it as a write. Above that, it must be a **pull (read-time merge)**. That single fact is why the architecture is a **hybrid**, not pure push.

> Storage of post *metadata* is a non-issue — about 30 TB/year with replication, a rounding error next to media. The real money is **CDN egress (~$18M/mo)** and **media at rest (~64 PB/yr)**.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is the read tide — about 350 thousand feed reads a second at peak against only about a thousand writes, so call it 300 reads to every write. That tells me to spend everything making reads cheap and do the expensive work on the write side. The second number that drives the whole design is the celebrity: one post from a 400-million-follower account is 400 million feed writes — so I will never fan that out on write. That's why I'm going hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrities."

---

## 4. Core Entities

> This is my first-draft list; I'll add full fields in the data model.

**User** — owns identity, profile, and follower/following counts.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of it like a person's library card — it identifies them and tracks what they're subscribed to."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you store follower counts for someone with 400M followers?" → "Not a live `COUNT(*)` — a denormalized, eventually-consistent counter updated asynchronously."

**Follow (edge)** — owns the directed graph edge `follower → followee`.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a one-way subscription: I subscribe to your channel; you don't automatically get mine."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you find all followers of a celebrity fast?" → "Indexed by followee; but we deliberately don't iterate them on every celeb post — that's the pull path."

**Post** — owns caption, author, timestamp, and references to media blobs (not the bytes).
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a catalog card pointing at the actual photo in the warehouse — the card is tiny, the warehouse is the blob store."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Where do the photo bytes live?" → "In object storage; the Post row only holds blob keys and rendition URLs, never the bytes."

**Media (blob + renditions)** — owns the actual file and its transcoded sizes (thumbnail, feed, full).
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "The warehouse shelf holding the original photo plus several pre-cut sizes ready to ship."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Why multiple renditions?" → "So the feed serves a 0.3 MB thumbnail, not a 5 MB original — that's most of the CDN savings."

**FeedEntry (timeline cache)** — owns a single user's pre-computed list of `(post_id, score, ts)`.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a personalized mailbox we keep pre-stuffed so opening the app is just reading your mailbox, not searching the whole post office."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Is this the source of truth?" → "No — it's a derived cache; we can always rebuild it from Posts + Follows."

**Interaction (Like / Comment)** — owns who liked/commented on what, plus the aggregate counters.
- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A guestbook entry plus a running tally on the cover."
- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Exact or approximate counts?" → "Sharded counters, approximate for display; the user's own like state is exact via a per-user set."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are User, the Follow edge, Post, Media, the FeedEntry timeline cache, and Interactions for likes and comments. The one I'd flag as the most important and most misunderstood is FeedEntry — it's not a source of truth, it's a derived, pre-computed mailbox we can always rebuild. I'll fill in the full field-level schema in the data-model section."

---

## 5. API / Interface

> Default REST over HTTPS. The caller is always identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token in the request header that proves who you are — the server trusts the token, never a user id in the body)*. We never trust client-supplied ids, timestamps, or counts.

**5.1 Upload a post** — `POST /v1/posts`
- *Why POST:* it creates new server state (a new post).
- Key request fields: `mediaUploadIds: string[]` — references to already-uploaded blobs (see 5.2); `caption: string`; `idempotencyKey: string` — dedups retries so a flaky network can't create two posts. The client does **not** send `authorId` (taken from the JWT) or `createdAt` (server clock).
```
// Request
POST /v1/posts
Headers: Authorization: Bearer <jwt>, Idempotency-Key: 9f2c-uuid
{ "mediaUploadIds": ["upl_a1", "upl_a2"], "caption": "sunset 🌅" }

// Response 202
{ "postId": "post_7Yk", "status": "PROCESSING", "createdAt": "2026-06-21T18:04:00Z" }
```
- **Key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted / PROCESSING**, not 200 — transcoding and fan-out are async. `authorId` and `status` are server-set; the client can't forge them.

**5.2 Get an upload URL** — `POST /v1/media:initUpload`
- *Why POST:* it allocates a server resource (a signed upload slot).
- Returns a **pre-signed URL** *(a temporary, permissioned link that lets the phone upload bytes straight to object storage, bypassing our servers)*.
```
// Request
POST /v1/media:initUpload
{ "contentType": "image/jpeg", "byteSize": 4200000 }

// Response 200
{ "uploadId": "upl_a1", "uploadUrl": "https://blob.cdn/...sig...", "expiresIn": 600 }
```
- **Key design decision:** the bytes never traverse our app servers — the phone PUTs directly to the blob store. This is the single biggest scalability win on the write path.

**5.3 Follow a user** — `PUT /v1/users/{targetId}/follow`
- *Why PUT:* idempotent — following twice is the same as following once.
```
// Request:  PUT /v1/users/u_celeb42/follow
// Response 200
{ "following": true, "since": "2026-06-21T18:04:10Z" }
```
- **Key design decision:** idempotent by nature, so no idempotency key needed; `followerId` comes from the JWT.

**5.4 Get home feed** — `GET /v1/feed?cursor=<opaque>&limit=20`
- *Why GET:* read-only.
- Key params: `cursor` — opaque pagination token (encodes position + freshness watermark) so infinite scroll is stable even as new posts arrive; `limit`.
```
// Request:  GET /v1/feed?cursor=eyJ0cyI6MTcuLi59&limit=20
// Response 200
{ "items": [
    { "postId": "post_7Yk", "authorId": "u_99", "caption": "sunset 🌅",
      "media": [{ "url": "https://cdn/feed/post_7Yk_a1.jpg", "w": 1080 }],
      "likeCount": 1243, "youLiked": false, "createdAt": "2026-06-21T18:04:00Z" }
  ],
  "nextCursor": "eyJ0cyI6MTcuLi4xfQ" }
```
- **Key design decision:** the server merges the pre-computed push feed with freshly-pulled celebrity posts here; `youLiked` is resolved per-caller so the feed is read-your-own-writes correct for your own like state.

**5.5 Like a post** — `PUT /v1/posts/{postId}/like`
- *Why PUT:* idempotent — liking twice still means "liked once."
```
// Request:  PUT /v1/posts/post_7Yk/like
// Response 200
{ "liked": true, "likeCount": 1244 }
```
- **Key design decision:** idempotent on `(userId, postId)`; the returned `likeCount` is approximate (sharded counter), but `liked` is exact for the caller.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends authorId, price-equivalents, or counts — those all come from the server. The phone uploads bytes straight to blob storage via a pre-signed URL, and post creation returns 202 because the real work is async."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The API is plain REST. To post, the phone first asks for a pre-signed upload URL and PUTs the bytes straight to object storage — they never hit our app servers, which is the big write-path win. Then it calls POST /posts with just the upload ids and a caption; we take the author from the JWT, return 202 PROCESSING, and do transcoding and fan-out asynchronously. Follow and like are idempotent PUTs. Reading the feed is a GET with an opaque cursor, and we resolve your own like-state per-request so you always see your own actions reflected."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

> Color language used in every diagram: blue = client apps, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores (cylinders), purple = external (CDN/object store).

### 6.1 "Upload media (create a post)"

The interesting part isn't storing a file — it's getting big bytes off the app servers and kicking off async transcode + fan-out.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Object store (S3-class) | A bucket that stores arbitrarily large files cheaply and durably | 11-nines durability + pre-signed direct upload keeps petabytes off our DB and our app servers | Not Postgres BLOBs (a DB is the wrong place for 64 PB; bloats backups); not NFS (no global durability) | Eventual visibility of new keys; no transactions across objects |
| Upload service | A thin service that mints pre-signed URLs and records post metadata | Lets the phone upload directly, so our servers only handle tiny metadata | Not proxying bytes through app (would need huge bandwidth + memory) | Must validate the object exists/scan it after the fact |
| Post metadata DB | A sharded relational store for post rows | Need indexed queries by author and time, modest size (~30 TB/yr) | Not a pure KV (we query by author + time ranges) | Manual sharding by author_id |
| Media-processing queue | A durable log of "new media uploaded" events | Decouples slow transcoding from the fast user response | Not synchronous transcode (would block the 202 for seconds) | Feed/profile shows a placeholder until transcode finishes |
| Transcode workers | A pool that makes thumbnail/feed/full renditions | One 5 MB original becomes a 0.3 MB feed image — most CDN savings | Not on-the-fly resize at serve time (recompute every view = waste) | Storage of several renditions per post |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Object store (S3-class)** — a service that stores files (blobs) by key with extreme durability and cheap-per-GB pricing. Here it holds every photo/video and all renditions, so the DB never touches bytes.
> **Pre-signed URL** — a temporary signed link that grants the holder permission to upload one object directly. Here it lets phones bypass our servers entirely on upload.
> **Queue (durable log)** — a buffer that reliably holds events until a worker consumes them. Here it holds "media uploaded" events so transcoding can happen out of band without making the user wait.
> **Transcoding** — converting a media file into other sizes/formats. Here it produces the small feed rendition that saves most of the bandwidth bill.

**Diagram — Upload / create-post flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 initUpload"| Upload[Upload service]
    Upload -->|"2 presigned url"| Client
    Client -->|"3 PUT bytes"| Blob[(Object store)]
    Client -->|"4 POST post meta"| Upload
    Upload -->|"5 write row"| PostDB[(Post metadata DB)]
    Upload -.->|"6 emit media event"| MQ[[Media queue]]
    MQ -.->|"7 consume"| Worker[Transcode workers]
    Worker -->|"8 write renditions"| Blob
    Worker -.->|"9 emit post-ready"| FanQ[[Fan-out queue]]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#2e1a3c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3

    class MQ,FanQ queue

```

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client calls initUpload.** It goes to the Upload service because that service owns minting signed slots. State: an `uploadId` is reserved. If it fails, the client simply retries — nothing durable yet.
2. **Service returns a pre-signed URL.** Why here: only the server holds the signing key. State: none yet. Risk: an expired URL → client re-inits.
3. **Client PUTs bytes straight to the object store.** Why direct: keeps 64 PB/yr of bytes off our app servers. State: the blob now exists. Risk: partial upload → the object is incomplete; we only reference it after step 4 validates size.
4. **Client POSTs post metadata** (upload ids + caption). Why Upload service: it owns post rows. State: a Post row in `PROCESSING`. Risk: duplicate POST → the idempotency key dedups.
5. **Service writes the Post row** to the sharded metadata DB, sharded by author. State: durable post record. Risk: shard down → retry with backoff; client already has its 202.
6. **Service emits a "media uploaded" event** to the media queue (fire-and-forget). State: event durably enqueued. Risk: queue down → upload service buffers/retries; user still sees PROCESSING.
7–8. **Transcode workers consume and write renditions** (thumbnail/feed/full) back to the object store. State: renditions exist; post can be marked READY. Risk: a bad file → mark FAILED, notify user to retry.
9. **Worker emits "post-ready"** to the fan-out queue — this hands off to §6.3.

> 💬 "The trick here is the bytes never touch my servers — the phone uploads straight to the bucket, and everything slow happens after a fast 202."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On upload, the phone first grabs a pre-signed URL and uploads the bytes directly to object storage — that keeps tens of petabytes a year completely off my app servers. Then it posts just the metadata, I write a tiny post row, return 202 immediately, and drop an event on a queue. Transcode workers pick that up, make the small renditions that save most of my bandwidth bill, and then signal that the post is ready to fan out. The trade-off is the post is briefly in a PROCESSING state, which is invisible to the user in practice."

### 6.2 "Follow / unfollow"

A tiny write, but it's the input to feed assembly and it tells us whether the target is a celebrity.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Graph/edge store | A store of directed follow edges, indexed both ways | Need "who do I follow" (feed build) and "who follows X" (fan-out) | Not a graph DB (overkill — we only need 1-hop edges); not a single index (need both directions) | Two indexes to keep in sync |
| Follower-count counter | A denormalized, async-updated tally per user | Live COUNT over 400M edges is impossible per request | Not on-read COUNT(*) (scans millions of rows) | Counts are eventually consistent |
| Celebrity flag | A boolean/threshold marking high-follower accounts | Lets the feed pick push vs pull per author | Not recomputed per post (cache the flag) | Flag lags reality by minutes near the threshold |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Edge store** — a store of relationship rows (follower → followee) indexed in both directions. Here it answers both "whose posts go in my feed" and "who should receive a normal user's post."
> **Denormalized counter** — a stored running total updated asynchronously instead of computed on demand. Here it serves the follower count without scanning the edge table.

**Diagram — Follow flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1 PUT follow"| FollowSvc[Follow service]
    FollowSvc -->|"2 upsert edge"| Edge[(Edge store)]
    FollowSvc -.->|"3 incr counters"| Counter[(Counter store)]
    FollowSvc -->|"4 check threshold"| CelebFlag[(Celebrity flag)]
    FollowSvc -.->|"5 backfill recent posts"| FeedCache[(Feed cache)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class FollowSvc svc
    class Edge,Counter,CelebFlag,FeedCache db
```
↳ reuses existing: Feed cache (introduced in §6.3).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client PUTs follow.** Goes to the Follow service which owns edges. State: a follow request. Risk: double-tap → idempotent upsert.
2. **Upsert the edge** (both directions indexed). State: graph edge exists. Risk: partial write → retry; edge store is the source of truth.
3. **Increment follower/following counters** async. State: counts trend correct. Risk: lost increment → periodic reconciliation job.
4. **Check the celebrity threshold** for the followee. State: maybe flip the celeb flag. Risk: thundering follows near threshold → debounce the flag flip.
5. **Optionally backfill** the follower's feed cache with the followee's recent posts so the feed isn't empty right after following. State: feed cache warmed. Risk: skip for celebrities (we pull them at read time anyway).

> 💬 "Following is trivial mechanically, but it's where I decide push-vs-pull — by checking if the target is a celebrity."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Follow is a small idempotent write into an edge store I index in both directions — one direction builds your feed, the other tells me who should receive a normal user's posts. I bump denormalized counters asynchronously instead of counting rows live, and I check a cached celebrity flag, because that flag is what later decides whether a post gets pushed to followers or pulled at read time."

### 6.3 "Home feed (timeline of followees' posts)"

This is the read hot path at ~350K/s and the heart of the design: a **hybrid** of push (pre-computed) and pull (live-merged) feeds.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed cache (Redis) | An in-memory store holding each user's pre-built timeline list | Reading your mailbox is O(1) and sub-ms; absorbs the 350K/s tide | Not query-on-read for everyone (would hammer the DB at 350K/s) | Memory cost; it's a derived cache, must be rebuildable |
| Fan-out service + queue | Workers that copy a new post into followers' feed caches | Pre-computing on the cheap write side keeps reads trivial | Not synchronous fan-out (a celeb post would block) | Eventual feed freshness (seconds) |
| Feed assembly service | Merges the push list with freshly-pulled celebrity posts, then ranks | Hybrid avoids the celeb write bomb while keeping normal feeds pre-built | Not pure pull (would be too slow for everyone) | Read-time merge cost for celeb posts only |
| Ranking scorer (pluggable) | Re-orders candidate posts by a score | Lets us swap chrono → ML without touching the pipeline | Not hard-coded chrono (product wants ranking) | Treated as a black box here |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Fan-out-on-write (push)** — when you post, we immediately copy a reference into each follower's pre-built feed. Here it's used for normal users so reads are just a cache fetch.
> **Fan-out-on-read (pull)** — at feed-read time we fetch a few celebrities' recent posts live and merge them in. Here it avoids writing one celeb post to 400M feeds.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value/data-structure store, microsecond-fast. Here it holds each user's timeline as a sorted list keyed by user id.

**Diagram — Hybrid feed (read + fan-out paths)**

    Client -.->|"async post-ready"| FanQ[[Fan-out queue]]

        FeedSvc -->|"3 pull celeb posts"| PostDB[(Post metadata DB)]
        FeedSvc -->|"4 rank merge"| Ranker[Ranking scorer]
        FeedSvc -->|"5 hydrate media urls"| Blob[(Object store)]
    end
    subgraph WritePath["Fan-out path"]
        FanQ -.->|"6 consume"| FanSvc[Fan-out service]
        FanSvc -.->|"7 push to follower feeds"| FeedCache
    end
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3

↳ reuses existing: Post metadata DB, Object store (§6.1), Fan-out queue (§6.1 step 9).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client GETs feed.** Hits the Feed assembly service, which owns merge+rank. State: none. Risk: cold start → fall through to a rebuild from edges.
2. **Read the pre-pushed list** from the feed cache (the user's mailbox). State: candidate set of normal-follow posts. Risk: cache miss → rebuild from edge store + recent posts.
3. **Pull recent posts of the user's followed celebrities** live from the post DB (a handful of authors). State: celeb candidates added. Risk: celeb DB hot → these are themselves cached per-celebrity (§8).
4. **Rank/merge** the combined candidates. State: ordered feed page. Risk: ranker slow/down → fall back to chronological.
5. **Hydrate media URLs** (rendition URLs, not bytes) and return. State: response sent. Risk: missing rendition → serve placeholder.
6–7. **Asynchronously**, when a normal user posts, the fan-out service consumes "post-ready" and **pushes the post id into each follower's feed cache list**. State: followers' mailboxes updated. Risk: a borderline-celebrity → skip push, rely on pull.

> 💬 "The whole feed is a hybrid: I pre-stuff normal users' mailboxes on write, but I pull celebrity posts live at read time and merge — that one decision dodges the 400-million-write bomb."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The feed is a hybrid. For normal users I fan out on write — when you post, I copy a tiny reference into each follower's pre-built list in Redis, so reading the feed is just fetching your mailbox, sub-millisecond, which is how I survive 350 thousand reads a second. But for celebrities I do the opposite: I never push their posts; instead, when you open your feed I pull the handful of celebrities you follow live and merge them in. Then a pluggable ranker orders the page. The trade-off is the feed is eventually consistent — fresh within seconds — which the product is fine with."
